assumptions: The dividend has been declared and recognised as an intragroup receivable/payable, so the item is a recognised monetary asset/liability rather than a forecast transaction.
The analysis is for consolidated financial statements and not for separate or individual financial statements.
The group is not an investment entity, because no facts indicate the specific IFRS 10 exception mentioned in IFRS 9.
No facts indicate that the dividend receivable is being treated as part of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 generally limits hedged items in consolidation to exposures with parties external to the reporting entity. Intragroup assets, liabilities and transactions are therefore normally excluded.
approaches.practical_implication: A booked intragroup dividend receivable cannot be hedge-accounted in consolidation unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 states that only items with external parties may be hedged in consolidated financial statements, subject to specific exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of a recognised intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 specifically allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to be a hedged item in consolidation if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: the dividend receivable/payable is a recognised intragroup monetary item
the relevant group entities have different functional currencies so FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
only the foreign currency risk is designated as the hedged risk
the hedge relationship meets IFRS 9 documentation and effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: This is the main route by which a declared intragroup dividend receivable may qualify for hedge accounting in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits hedging the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements when FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness criteria to be met.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a separate exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss. That model does not fit once the dividend has already been recognised as a receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not analyse the recognised dividend receivable under the forecast-transaction exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows certain forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged in consolidation if denominated in a non-functional currency and affecting consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraphs B6.3.5-B6.3.6 explain that only forecast intragroup transactions affecting consolidated profit or loss qualify; many internal cash flows do not.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.reasoning: A net investment hedge is a distinct hedge accounting model for foreign operations. IFRIC 16 says it applies only to net investment hedges and should not be used by analogy for other intragroup exposures.
approaches.practical_implication: A dividend receivable should not be supported by the net-investment hedge model unless facts show it is part of a qualifying net investment exposure.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge type and hedged item category.
Paragraphs 7-8 restrict the interpretation to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and prohibit analogy to other hedge types.
